title: Maggie O'Farrell on Adapting Hamnet for the Screen
text: O'Farrell initially hesitated about the project before Zhao convinced her. The screenwriter explained: I remember her holding up the book saying, 'I want to make a film.' Both filmmakers shared cinematic influences, including Wong Kar-Wai and Terrence Malick. Zhao communicated primarily through voice notes—sometimes sending recordings lasting nearly an hour—while O'Farrell preferred written approaches to problem-solving. The biggest structural change involved reorganizing the narrative. The original 350-page book shifts between timelines, but the filmmakers reconstructed it chronologically to avoid jarring cinema audiences. O'Farrell noted that adapting required learning cinematic language, as things that work on the page don't really work on the screen. The opening scene of Agnes embracing a tree exemplifies how the filmmakers conveyed internal emotions visually. Rather than revealing inner thoughts as novels do, they used environmental storytelling and sound design to communicate character feelings. O'Farrell wrote the novel to challenge negative historical narratives about Anne Hathaway. She conducted extensive research—visiting Stratford, learning falconry, cultivating Elizabethan gardens, and baking Tudor bread—to authentically understand Shakespeare's wife's world.
